evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-JSON translation for exploration results; HTTP handlers call populateExploreResponse/populateComparisonResponse and do not inline collocate formatting

ExplorationService interface provides a minimal, honest seam (4 methods) between the HTTP layer and the exploration implementation; ExplorationHandlers depends only on the interface, not the concrete class
Options DTOs (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) make boundary inputs explicit and typed rather than passing loose primitive bags
GrammarConfigSerializer is a dedicated translation layer so GrammarConfig/RelationConfig remain pure data carriers with no JSON dependency
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a uniform, predictable error-propagation wrapper that every route registration uses consistently
resolveRelationConfig in ExplorationHandlers checks isEmpty() then calls orElseThrow() on line 265 — the terminal orElseThrow is unreachable dead code that obscures the intent

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges has a runtime branch: if perSeedCollocates is non-empty use it, else fall back to iterating seeds × collocates — this backward-compat fork means two structurally different ExplorationResult shapes flow through the same assembler path silently

handleSemanticFieldComparison assembles the response skeleton (status, seeds, seed_count, parameters) inline rather than delegating to buildCoreExploreResponse — asymmetric with the two sibling explore handlers that share a common helper

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples assembles the examples list inline with HashMap/ArrayList, a pattern that ExploreResponseAssembler standardised for exploration responses but was not extended to cover concordance responses

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler is a single, explicit translation seam: all model-to-response mapping for exploration lives there, not scattered across handlers
ExplorationService interface is minimal and honest — four methods that map directly to the four HTTP endpoint concerns, with no leakage of implementation details
Typed options DTOs (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) make boundary inputs explicit; no multi-parameter loose primitives crossing the HTTP→exploration seam
GrammarConfigSerializer correctly separates JSON-serialization concern from the config data model, keeping GrammarConfig and RelationConfig dependency-free
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides uniform error-propagation across all route registrations with a single, predictable pattern
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated inconsistencies in boundary discipline rather than structural seam failures: a silent legacy-compat fork in ExploreResponseAssembler, a redundant Optional unwrap in resolveRelationConfig, and asymmetric inline assembly in handleSemanticFieldComparison versus its sibling handlers.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The handoffs across the API→exploration and config→API boundaries are intentionally designed and mostly clean: the ExplorationService interface, ExploreResponseAssembler, and options DTOs are all good seam design. The codebase sits in the 'solid but uneven' range (80=solid) rather than exemplary (95+) because three real friction points exist: the silent dual-path in buildExplorationEdges degrades transparency at a boundary, the comparison handler assembles its own skeleton rather than using the shared builder, and the redundant orElseThrow in resolveRelationConfig is a minor but visible seam smell. These are all fixable without architectural change, which limits the severity, placing the score comfortably in the low-to-mid 70s.

issues.summary: Silent dual-path in ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges allows two different ExplorationResult shapes without making the distinction explicit at the boundary
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler line 41: `if (!perSeed.isEmpty()) { ... } else { // Legacy fallback }` — the assembler silently handles two structurally different ExplorationResult instances
ExplorationResult exposes two public constructors: one with perSeedCollocates and one without (defaults to Map.of()), making the 'legacy' shape easy to produce accidentally
The legacy fallback duplicates seeds × collocates in a cartesian product that is semantically wrong for multi-seed results where each seed has distinct collocate sets
issues.suggestion: Remove the no-perSeedCollocates constructor from ExplorationResult and require all callers to supply the per-seed map; delete the legacy fallback branch in buildExplorationEdges. If backward compat is required short-term, at minimum throw in the fallback branch rather than silently executing incorrect multi-seed edge attribution.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison assembles the response skeleton inline instead of using buildCoreExploreResponse, breaking symmetry with sibling handlers
evidence: handleSemanticFieldComparison (line ~141): `response.put("status", "ok"); ... paramsUsed.put("relation", "all"); ...` — four response fields assembled inline
handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both delegate to buildCoreExploreResponse for the same fields
The comparison handler notes in its Javadoc that it 'intentionally does not share the seed/relation parsing preamble' but this doesn't justify the response assembly asymmetry
issues.suggestion: Have handleSemanticFieldComparison call buildCoreExploreResponse (or a variant) for the shared response scaffold, passing Map.of("relation", "all") as extraParams; this keeps the three handlers symmetric at the response-building seam.

issues.summary: resolveRelationConfig checks isEmpty() then calls orElseThrow() — the terminal unwrap is unreachable and obscures the error-propagation contract
evidence: Lines 257-265: `if (relationConfig.isEmpty()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException(...) } ... return relationConfig.orElseThrow();` — the orElseThrow on line 265 can never trigger because the isEmpty check on line 257 already throws
A reader must trace both paths to confirm the orElseThrow is dead code, adding unnecessary cognitive load at the method's return seam
issues.suggestion: Replace the isEmpty check + orElseThrow pattern with a single `orElseThrow(() -> new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown relation: " + relationId))`, then check relationType on the unwrapped value directly. This makes the error-propagation path readable in one pass.

root_causes: Backward-compatibility layering: ExplorationResult and its assembler accumulated a legacy compat path that was not cleaned up once all callers moved to the new perSeedCollocates shape
Cross-cutting response assembly was centralised for explore endpoints but the comparison handler was not refactored to match when it was added
